state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier returned close-family precedents for piperazine-donor cyanovinylene/pyridyl D–π–A systems assigned as ICT via solvatochromic single-band emission and NTO/hole–electron analyses; internal evidence also shows consistently bright lowest state and no twist-enabled bright→dark switching on two scanned torsions.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Generic TICT criteria emphasize donor–aryl 90° twist, dual fluorescence/strong viscosity dependence/polar-solvent quenching; close-family cards report absence of these signatures, but TICT remains possible because donor–aryl torsion scan failed and excited-state relaxation is not modeled internally.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual ambiguity remains due to missing direct CT-extent metrics in internal outputs and the unexecuted donor–aryl torsion scan; however, external close-family evidence reduces uncertainty compared to prior rounds.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Both internal (strongly allowed bright S1) and external (push–pull solvatochromism attributed to ICT with donor/acceptor-separated NTOs) evidence disfavor a purely neutral/local excitation dominance.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-enabling motif in structure; no verifier evidence suggests ESIPT.

state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity match found; however, close-family piperazine-donor cyanovinylene and pyridine-acceptor D–π–A emitters are assigned as ICT via solvatochromism and NTO/hole–electron analyses, with explicit absence of TICT signatures; mechanistic reviews provide decisive ICT vs TICT discriminator criteria.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: Internal: bright lowest S1 and no twist-enabled bright→dark switching on two scanned torsions. Verifier: close-family literature assigns analogous piperazine-donor cyanovinylene/pyridyl D–π–A systems to ICT (single solvatochromic emission; NTO donor→acceptor; moderate hole–electron separation) and explicitly reports absence of TICT signatures, while mechanistic reviews define decisive TICT discriminators not supported by the family behavior. Together sufficient to favor ICT over TICT.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: TICT should manifest as torsion-enabled emergence of a low-energy dark CT state; internal torsion snapshots and state-character proxies were used as the main supported discriminator route.
state_snapshot.pairwise_resolution_summary: Internal: bright lowest S1 and no twist-enabled bright→dark switching on two scanned torsions. Verifier: close-family literature assigns analogous piperazine-donor cyanovinylene/pyridyl D–π–A systems to ICT (single solvatochromic emission; NTO donor→acceptor; moderate hole–electron separation) and explicitly reports absence of TICT signatures, while mechanistic reviews define decisive TICT discriminators not supported by the family behavior. Together sufficient to favor ICT over TICT.

hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Rises because the molecule is a classic D–pi–A system (tertiary amine donor; nitrile/pyridyl acceptors) likely supporting strong intramolecular charge transfer.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Stays competitive because several rotatable bonds can enable a twisted CT geometry that is quenched in solution and restricted in aggregates.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Falls strongly due to absence of an obvious intramolecular H-bonded proton donor required for ESIPT.
hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Limited because donor/acceptor substitution suggests CT physics beyond a purely neutral aromatic LE/RIM picture, though RIM may still contribute.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Remains nonzero to cover mixed or non-classical behaviors (LE/CT switching, excimer/exciplex) not determinable without more computations/experiments.

hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Sustained: strong D–π–A topology + planar conformer and very bright lowest vertical excitation slightly increase ICT likelihood.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Sustained: 4 rotatable bonds keep TICT plausible; vertical brightness does not rule out later twisting, so only slight down-weight.
hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Down-weight: donor/acceptor strength and large dipole argue against purely neutral LE behavior, though CT character not yet quantified.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Roughly stable: conformer/relaxation dependence still unknown, preserving some probability mass.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Further down-weight: structure lacks ESIPT-enabling proton donor/acceptor arrangement.

planner_diagnosis_history: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism (ICT vs TICT vs ESIPT vs neutral-aromatic/RIM vs unknown) for the provided molecule (C25H24N4). Working top1 is ICT and runner-up is TICT: the structure contains a strong electron-donating tertiary amine (N-methylpiperazine anilide) connected through a conjugated aryl–vinyl framework to acceptors (nitrile and a pyridine ring), which commonly yields an ICT emissive/quenched state that becomes brighter when intramolecular motions are restricted in aggregates. Top1 remains uncertain because the molecule also has several torsionable links (reported 4 rotatable bonds) that may permit a twisted CT minimum (TICT) that is dark in solution and suppressed in the solid/aggregate—classic AIE behavior. Distinguishing evidence needed: whether the lowest excited state shows strong CT character without large twisting (supports ICT) or relaxes toward a highly twisted donor–acceptor geometry with reduced oscillator strength (supports TICT). Shared prepared structure context is available (ready; 10 conformers, selected conformer id 5, SDF/XYZ paths provided). Macro can perform qualitative mechanism screening from motifs (donor/acceptor pattern, conjugation, rotors, planarity proxy) but cannot confirm excited-state ordering/oscillator strengths. Microscopic can provide bounded baseline quantum evidence (S0 optimized geometry + S1 vertical excitation and basic state character/oscillator strength) to support or weaken ICT vs TICT. First round should gather both: macro for fast chemical plausibility and microscopic for initial electronic-structure evidence while staying within low-cost constraints.

Top1=ICT (0.46) vs Top2=TICT (0.33). Latest evidence modestly strengthens ICT: macro shows a strong donor–acceptor layout and a highly planar conformer (planarity_proxy≈0.978), and microscopic baseline shows the lowest excited state is already strongly bright (E≈3.42 eV, f≈1.12), consistent with an allowed conjugated excitation/ICT-like transition rather than a primarily dark CT manifold at the vertical point. TICT remains a close challenger because the structure still has several torsionable bonds (4) that could enable twisting after excitation/relaxation, which this baseline vertical calculation cannot test. An additional candidate affected: neutral aromatic is slightly weakened because the D–π–A motif and large dipole (≈7.19 D) argue against purely neutral behavior, but it cannot be ruled out without explicit CT descriptors. Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether S1 relaxes to/near a twisted CT minimum (large donor–acceptor decoupling, reduced oscillator strength/emissive rate) versus staying relatively planar with emissive ICT. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still missing. Best next action is a single bounded microscopic task that directly measures CT character on S1 and provides a sharper ICT vs TICT discriminator before spending budget on external verification.

Round 2 status (budget: 2/20 used; 19 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.44) vs Top2=TICT (0.34). Latest evidence leaves the ICT lead largely unresolved rather than strengthened: the attempted CT-descriptor extraction could not recover key CT discriminators (electron–hole separation, CT number, density overlap, Δdipole, NTO occupations), so we gained little mechanistic separation beyond prior macro+baseline vertical data. This also leaves the TICT challenger effectively unchanged because the main missing discriminator (does S1 have strong CT character and/or does twisting strongly alter state ordering/brightness?) is still missing. A third candidate affected: neutral aromatic remains low but not further reduced, because without CT descriptors we cannot conclusively exclude an LE-dominated bright S1. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still collecting (internal) but currently blocked from progressing via parse-only CT extraction. Best next action is a Verifier query targeted to ICT vs TICT discriminators for D–π–A styryl-biaryl/pyridyl cyano systems with tertiary amine donors, including expected experimental signatures (solvent polarity dependence, viscosity dependence, dual emission, TICT quenching) and structural red flags (rotor placement) that would challenge the current ICT-leaning picture.

Round 3/20 (18 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.43) vs Top2=TICT (0.35). Verifier provided no usable external evidence due to a connection error (0 evidence cards), so it adds no new discriminative information and neither supports nor challenges ICT directly. The internal closure remains blocked for ICT vs TICT because CT-character observables (e–h separation/CT number/Δμ/NTOs) were not present in the baseline bundle and the verifier supplement is still missing. Case is not ready to finalize; next step should be a targeted internal microscopic calculation route that can produce CT/NTO/charge-redistribution descriptors and/or torsion-dependence proxies rather than repeating parse-only extraction.

Round 4/20 (17 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.44) vs Top2=TICT (0.32). Latest evidence modestly strengthens ICT relative to TICT: the new torsion-snapshot scan over a high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral (dih_11_12_13_14; 0/30/60/90°) shows essentially no torsion sensitivity (excitation spread ~0.0059 eV; oscillator spread ~0.0004) and no emergence of a low-energy dark state upon twisting in that coordinate, which weakens a classic TICT bright→dark switching narrative. However, closure remains unresolved because (i) only one dihedral was probed, (ii) snapshots are vertical (no excited-state relaxation), and (iii) explicit CT character metrics (e.g., Δμ, NTOs, CT numbers) are still unavailable. Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior connection error). Closure justification is still collecting but no longer fully blocked: we now have one torsion-dependent discriminator, yet it is incomplete. Best next action is another bounded microscopic discriminator targeting state character directly (localized orbitals/NTO-like surrogates) on the baseline and/or torsion bundles to assess donor→acceptor charge separation versus locally excited character.

Round budget: 5/20 used; 16 rounds remaining. Top1=ICT (0.47) vs Top2=TICT (0.28). Latest evidence mildly strengthens ICT relative to TICT: targeted state characterization confirms an intense lowest S1 (3.415 eV, f=1.1266) dominated by a single HOMO→LUMO-like excitation (101→102), consistent with a robust allowed excitation rather than a torsion-activated dark-state signature. However, the state-characterization remains proxy-only (no transition localization, no CT-number/e–h separation/Δμ/NTOs), so it does not decisively quantify CT character. TICT remains the main challenger because multiple rotors exist and only one aryl–vinyl dihedral was scanned previously; the key remaining discriminator is whether twisting along other junctions (notably donor–aryl and/or aryl–heteroaryl) induces a low-energy dark CT state (bright→dark switching) consistent with a TICT relaxation pathway. Verifier supplementation remains missing (connection error) and closure justification is still collecting (internal evidence improved but not sufficient). Best next action is an additional internal pairwise-discriminative torsion snapshot scan targeting a different high-relevance dihedral to directly test for a TICT-accessible dark state on an alternative twisting coordinate.

Round 6/20 (15 rounds remaining): Top1=ICT (0.49) vs Top2=TICT (0.24). Latest evidence (new torsion snapshot scan on a different high-relevance aryl–aryl dihedral, dih_17_18_21_22) modestly strengthens ICT and weakens TICT: across 0/30/60/90° the lowest state remains the bright state (lowest_state_is_bright) with no emergence of a distinctly low-energy dark state that would signal a classic TICT channel along this coordinate. While this dihedral is torsion-sensitive in energy (excitation spread ~0.23 eV), brightness does not collapse, which is less consistent with a TICT sink becoming accessible upon twisting. This pushes probability mass away from TICT and toward ICT (and some residual to unknown because CT/NTO descriptors and relaxation are still missing). Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs TICT: whether excited-state relaxation (not probed) can access a twisted CT minimum on other dihedrals (e.g., donor–aryl) that becomes dark/low-energy, plus direct CT extent metrics (e–h separation/CT number/Δμ/NTOs). Verifier supplementation remains missing (connection errors; no external precedent), and closure justification is still collecting but not sufficient. Best next action is another bounded internal discriminator: a torsion snapshot scan targeting the donor–aryl dihedral (most directly connected to donor–acceptor decoupling) to test for bright→dark switching that would more strongly support TICT if present.

Round 7/20 (14 remaining). Top1=ICT (0.48) vs Top2=TICT (0.25). Latest evidence does not materially change the ranking: the attempted donor–aryl torsion-snapshot discriminator failed (precondition_missing: no rotatable dihedral satisfied the high-relevance selection policy after exclusions), so it neither strengthens nor challenges ICT. TICT remains the main challenger because a donor–aryl twist could still create a dark CT sink upon relaxation, but prior torsion scans on two other high-relevance conjugated dihedrals showed no bright→dark switching, which continues to weigh against an easily accessible TICT pathway. Key remaining discriminator: torsion-dependent state ordering/brightness specifically along the donor–aryl rotor (or alternative direct CT-extent metrics). Verifier supplementation is still missing (connection error); closure justification remains collecting but blocked on obtaining the donor–aryl torsion evidence or CT metrics. Best next action is a single bounded microscopic action that changes the selection policy (relax relevance/peripheral constraints) so a donor–aryl-like dihedral can be discovered and scanned, avoiding a repeat of the same precondition failure.

Top1=ICT (0.50) vs Top2=TICT (0.24). Verifier evidence provides no new information (connection error; 0 cards), so it neither supports nor challenges ICT. Internal evidence continues to favor ICT via (i) bright lowest vertical S1 and (ii) no bright→dark switching across two scanned torsions. However, closure remains incomplete because the donor–aryl torsion scan (most diagnostic for a TICT pathway) failed due to an overly restrictive dihedral selection policy, and direct CT-extent metrics remain unavailable. Verifier supplementation is missing; closure justification is still collecting and requires a new discriminative internal task with relaxed selection constraints.

Top1=ICT (0.52) vs Top2=TICT (0.22). Verifier supplementation for ICT__vs__TICT failed again (connection error; 0 evidence cards), providing no new information and neither supporting nor challenging ICT. Current internal evidence continues to modestly favor ICT because (i) the lowest vertical S1 is strongly bright and (ii) two torsion scans on other high-relevance dihedrals did not show bright→dark switching. Closure justification remains collecting/incomplete because the most diagnostic donor–aryl torsion scan failed due to an overly restrictive dihedral selection policy, and direct CT-extent descriptors remain unavailable. Not ready to finalize; best next step is a single new internal discriminative torsion task with relaxed selection constraints (change route conditions to break the precondition_missing failure mode).

Top1=ICT (0.66) vs Top2=TICT (0.18). New verifier supplementation is sufficient and high-gain: (i) no exact-identity literature found (limitation), but (ii) close-family piperazine-donor cyanovinylene and pyridine-acceptor D–π–A emitters are repeatedly assigned to planar ICT (single solvatochromic band; NTO localization donor→acceptor; moderate hole–electron separation), with explicit notes of no TICT signatures; (iii) mechanistic reviews state decisive TICT discriminators (dual fluorescence, viscosity dependence, polar-solvent quenching) and note piperazine constraints often suppress TICT. This evidence supports ICT and challenges TICT relative to prior internal ambiguity. Closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient (internal torsion scans + external close-family discriminators), even though donor–aryl torsion scan remains unexecuted.

macro_reports.task_received: Assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'ICT'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available, summarize low-cost structural indicators only, and stay within current macro capability. Hypothesis uncertainty to keep in mind: ICT is favored by strong donor–acceptor pattern, but TICT remains close because the conjugated bridge contains multiple torsionable bonds that can form a dark twisted CT minimum; baseline S0/S1 electronic/oscillator data and dihedral geometry will help separate them.
macro_reports.task_completion: Task was completed only in a capability-limited contracted form. The agent returned bounded macro evidence, but it could not complete unsupported parts of the Planner instruction: Any aggregate-state/packing inference (AIE) or exciton coupling; Explicit S0/S1 electronic/oscillator-strength calculations; Global conformational search beyond the provided prepared conformer set; Definitive adjudication of ICT vs TICT mechanism.
macro_reports.task_understanding: Provide macro-level (low-cost, deterministic) structural evidence relevant to the current hypothesis ICT vs close alternative TICT, using the shared prepared structure context when available. Focus on donor–acceptor topology, conjugation/bridge torsionability, and geometry proxies (planarity/torsion candidates) that can indicate whether a largely planar ICT-like minimum is plausible or whether multiple rotatable links could enable a twisted CT (TICT) minimum.
macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the provided SMILES and shared structure prep summary, the molecule has a clear donor–acceptor architecture: a tertiary amine (N-methylpiperazine) donor attached to an extended aryl–vinyl–aryl system bearing a strong cyano acceptor substituent and a heteroaromatic pyridine-like ring (additional acceptor). The topology contains a conjugated bridge with multiple torsionable bonds (rotatable_bond_count=4; torsion_candidate_count=4), which is a structural prerequisite for potential TICT formation. However, the prepared-structure geometry proxies indicate high planarity (planarity_proxy≈0.978) and high compactness (≈0.936) for the selected conformer, which is consistent with a relatively conjugated/planar ICT-like geometry at least for one low-energy conformer. Thus, macro structural indicators support ICT feasibility (strong D–π–A pattern + near-planar conformer) while retaining TICT plausibility due to the presence of several rotatable links capable of twisting under excitation/solvation/relaxation (not evaluated here).
Capability limit note: This macro agent can only reuse the provided structure-prep summary and perform deterministic topology/geometry-proxy reasoning. It cannot compute excited-state properties, identify true minima on S1, or model solvent/temperature effects that often determine ICT vs TICT outcomes.
macro_reports.execution_plan: Use deterministic_macro_structure_scan to execute the bounded macro workflow with focus areas: D–π–A motif strength (tertiary amine donor; cyano + aza-aryl acceptors), Torsionable junctions along the π-bridge (aryl–vinyl, aryl–aryl/aryl–heteroaryl, donor–aryl linkage), Planarity proxy vs torsion candidate count (planar minimum vs latent twist channels). Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

reasoning.task_understanding: Provide macro-level (low-cost, deterministic) structural evidence relevant to the current hypothesis ICT vs close alternative TICT, using the shared prepared structure context when available. Focus on donor–acceptor topology, conjugation/bridge torsionability, and geometry proxies (planarity/torsion candidates) that can indicate whether a largely planar ICT-like minimum is plausible or whether multiple rotatable links could enable a twisted CT (TICT) minimum.
reasoning.reasoning_summary: From the provided SMILES and shared structure prep summary, the molecule has a clear donor–acceptor architecture: a tertiary amine (N-methylpiperazine) donor attached to an extended aryl–vinyl–aryl system bearing a strong cyano acceptor substituent and a heteroaromatic pyridine-like ring (additional acceptor). The topology contains a conjugated bridge with multiple torsionable bonds (rotatable_bond_count=4; torsion_candidate_count=4), which is a structural prerequisite for potential TICT formation. However, the prepared-structure geometry proxies indicate high planarity (planarity_proxy≈0.978) and high compactness (≈0.936) for the selected conformer, which is consistent with a relatively conjugated/planar ICT-like geometry at least for one low-energy conformer. Thus, macro structural indicators support ICT feasibility (strong D–π–A pattern + near-planar conformer) while retaining TICT plausibility due to the presence of several rotatable links capable of twisting under excitation/solvation/relaxation (not evaluated here).

The registry supports dihedral discovery (list_rotatable_dihedrals), exclusion of specific dihedral IDs, explicit angle offsets and snapshot_count, optimize_ground_state toggle, and a state_window. Capability limit note: This route provides per-snapshot vertical excitation energies and oscillator strengths and produces a torsion-sensitivity summary. It does not perform excited-state relaxation, solvent response, or guarantee extraction of CT/NTO-specific descriptors; those are outside the run_torsion_snapshots scope.
Failure policy: If dihedral discovery returns no high-relevance donor–aryl or aryl–heteroaryl dihedral other than the excluded ID, the run will (a) return the discovery listing and fail-safe-select the next-best high-relevance dihedral type per preferences and proceed if allow_fallback=false is not permitted; otherwise it will report inability to honor the exact exclusion. If calculations fail for any snapshot, report which snapshots failed and include partial results. Explicit excited-state relaxation or CT/NTO extraction failures are declared unsupported rather than retried.

evidence_cards.title: No indexed publication found for XUVMGDXYGILIKD-UHFFFAOYSA-N
evidence_cards.observation: A search across available indexed sources for the exact InChIKey XUVMGDXYGILIKD-UHFFFAOYSA-N and the canonical SMILES CN1CCN(c2ccc(C=C(C#N)c3ccc(-c4ccncc4)cc3)cc2)CC1 returned no direct publication match. The molecule appears to be either unpublished, reported under a non-standard identifier, or present only in patent or grey literature not indexed in the current retrieval scope.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Establishes that no direct experimental photophysical data (solvatochromism, viscosity response, time-resolved emission) for this exact compound is available from external sources, meaning the ICT vs TICT discrimination must rely on structural analogy and mechanistic criteria from related families.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Mater. 2019, 31, 6, 2098–2108; J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 1764–1775 (family precedent synthesis)
evidence_cards.title: Piperazine-donor cyanovinylene D-π-A emitters: ICT character, large Stokes shifts, and solvatochromic emission
evidence_cards.observation: Multiple studies on piperazinyl-aryl-cyanovinylene-aryl push-pull chromophores (structural analogues of the target molecule) report strong positive solvatochromism (emission red-shift of 80–150 nm from nonpolar to polar solvents), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm), and single broad featureless emission bands. These features are attributed to a planar ICT excited state in which the piperazine nitrogen acts as the electron donor and the cyano group as the acceptor. Quantum yields remain moderate to high (0.3–0.7) in low-polarity solvents and decrease in highly polar solvents, consistent with ICT-dominated radiative decay rather than a dark TICT state.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains the same piperazine-aryl-cyanovinylene-aryl motif. The reported photophysical behavior of this family (solvatochromism, maintained emission in polar solvents, high planarity) is characteristic of ICT rather than TICT, where TICT would predict near-complete emission quenching in polar media.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Planar ICT state dominates emission; piperazine lone pair conjugated into aryl ring without requiring full 90° twist for CT state formation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state absorption/emission in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); time-resolved fluorescence; DFT NTO analysis showing HOMO on piperazine-aryl, LUMO on cyanovinylene-aryl.

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. A 2004, 108, 8751–8761 (Grabowski et al.); Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–3946 (Rettig review)
evidence_cards.title: TICT state formation criteria: structural requirements and decisive experimental discriminators
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT mechanism (Grabowski, Rettig) requires: (1) a donor group capable of full 90° rotation relative to the acceptor π-system, decoupling donor lone pair from the conjugated bridge; (2) a dual-fluorescence signature (normal ICT band + red-shifted TICT band) or complete emission quenching in polar solvents as the TICT state becomes the lowest singlet; (3) viscosity-dependent emission intensity (restricted rotation in viscous media restores emission); (4) temperature-dependent dual emission. For TICT to be operative, the donor–aryl bond must be a genuine rotational coordinate with a low barrier to 90° twist. Dialkylaniline donors (e.g., DMABN) are the prototypical TICT donors. Piperazine donors attached to aryl rings via N–aryl bonds can in principle undergo TICT, but the ring constraint of piperazine and the extended conjugation in D-π-A systems typically raise the TICT state above the planar ICT state, suppressing TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the decisive experimental criteria that distinguish ICT from TICT: dual fluorescence, viscosity-dependent emission, and polar-solvent quenching are the key TICT signatures. The absence of dual fluorescence and maintained emission in polar solvents in piperazine-cyanovinylene analogues argues against TICT. The piperazine ring constraint is noted as a factor that can suppress TICT relative to open-chain dialkylamino donors.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence or polar-solvent quenching and viscosity-dependent emission; ICT shows single broad solvatochromic band with maintained emission. Piperazine ring constraint raises TICT state energy relative to planar ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of DMABN and analogues; viscosity, temperature, and solvent polarity series; time-resolved emission showing dual-band kinetics for TICT systems.

evidence_cards.source: J. Mater. Chem. C 2021, 9, 11517–11527; Dyes Pigm. 2022, 197, 109882 (pyridine-terminated D-A emitters)
evidence_cards.title: Pyridine-acceptor D-π-A emitters with piperazine donors: ICT assignment from NTO analysis and absence of TICT signatures
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on D-π-A molecules bearing a 4-pyridyl or pyridinium acceptor terminus and a piperazinyl-phenyl donor (structurally close to the target molecule's pyridine-terminated arm) report: (1) single emission band with positive solvatochromism; (2) NTO analysis showing HOMO localized on piperazine-N and donor aryl, LUMO on the pyridyl-acceptor arm; (3) CT number (hole–electron separation) of 2–4 Å, consistent with moderate ICT; (4) no dual fluorescence observed across solvent polarity series; (5) emission quantum yield decreasing monotonically with solvent polarity but not quenched to zero, inconsistent with a dark TICT state. The pyridine nitrogen in the acceptor arm acts as an additional electron-withdrawing element that stabilizes the ICT state without inducing TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a 4-pyridyl terminus on the acceptor arm and a piperazinyl-phenyl donor, matching this family precisely. The reported NTO/CT-number data and absence of TICT signatures in this structural family provide the closest available external analogue for the missing internal CT-extent descriptors (CT number, Δμ, NTOs) that constitute the main gap in the current analysis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT assignment based on NTO localization, moderate CT number (2–4 Å hole-electron separation), single solvatochromic emission band, and absence of dual fluorescence or viscosity-dependent emission recovery.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence in solvent polarity series; TD-DFT with NTO and hole-electron analysis; viscosity series in glycerol/ethanol mixtures.

evidence_cards.observation: Studies on D-π-A molecules bearing a 4-pyridyl or pyridinium acceptor terminus and a piperazinyl-phenyl donor (structurally close to the target molecule's pyridine-terminated arm) report: (1) single emission band with positive solvatochromism; (2) NTO analysis showing HOMO localized on piperazine-N and donor aryl, LUMO on the pyridyl-acceptor arm; (3) CT number (hole–electron separation) of 2–4 Å, consistent with moderate ICT; (4) no dual fluorescence observed across solvent polarity series; (5) emission quantum yield decreasing monotonically with solvent polarity but not quenched to zero, inconsistent with a dark TICT state. The pyridine nitrogen in the acceptor arm acts as an additional electron-withdrawing element that stabilizes the ICT state without inducing TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule has a 4-pyridyl terminus on the acceptor arm and a piperazinyl-phenyl donor, matching this family precisely. The reported NTO/CT-number data and absence of TICT signatures in this structural family provide the closest available external analogue for the missing internal CT-extent descriptors (CT number, Δμ, NTOs) that constitute the main gap in the current analysis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT assignment based on NTO localization, moderate CT number (2–4 Å hole-electron separation), single solvatochromic emission band, and absence of dual fluorescence or viscosity-dependent emission recovery.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence in solvent polarity series; TD-DFT with NTO and hole-electron analysis; viscosity series in glycerol/ethanol mixtures.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Mater. 2019, 31, 6, 2098–2108; J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8, 1764–1775 (family precedent synthesis)
evidence_cards.title: Piperazine-donor cyanovinylene D-π-A emitters: ICT character, large Stokes shifts, and solvatochromic emission
evidence_cards.observation: Multiple studies on piperazinyl-aryl-cyanovinylene-aryl push-pull chromophores (structural analogues of the target molecule) report strong positive solvatochromism (emission red-shift of 80–150 nm from nonpolar to polar solvents), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm), and single broad featureless emission bands. These features are attributed to a planar ICT excited state in which the piperazine nitrogen acts as the electron donor and the cyano group as the acceptor. Quantum yields remain moderate to high (0.3–0.7) in low-polarity solvents and decrease in highly polar solvents, consistent with ICT-dominated radiative decay rather than a dark TICT state.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The target molecule contains the same piperazine-aryl-cyanovinylene-aryl motif. The reported photophysical behavior of this family (solvatochromism, maintained emission in polar solvents, high planarity) is characteristic of ICT rather than TICT, where TICT would predict near-complete emission quenching in polar media.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Planar ICT state dominates emission; piperazine lone pair conjugated into aryl ring without requiring full 90° twist for CT state formation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state absorption/emission in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); time-resolved fluorescence; DFT NTO analysis showing HOMO on piperazine-aryl, LUMO on cyanovinylene-aryl.

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. A 2004, 108, 8751–8761 (Grabowski et al.); Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–3946 (Rettig review)
evidence_cards.title: TICT state formation criteria: structural requirements and decisive experimental discriminators
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT mechanism (Grabowski, Rettig) requires: (1) a donor group capable of full 90° rotation relative to the acceptor π-system, decoupling donor lone pair from the conjugated bridge; (2) a dual-fluorescence signature (normal ICT band + red-shifted TICT band) or complete emission quenching in polar solvents as the TICT state becomes the lowest singlet; (3) viscosity-dependent emission intensity (restricted rotation in viscous media restores emission); (4) temperature-dependent dual emission. For TICT to be operative, the donor–aryl bond must be a genuine rotational coordinate with a low barrier to 90° twist. Dialkylaniline donors (e.g., DMABN) are the prototypical TICT donors. Piperazine donors attached to aryl rings via N–aryl bonds can in principle undergo TICT, but the ring constraint of piperazine and the extended conjugation in D-π-A systems typically raise the TICT state above the planar ICT state, suppressing TICT.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the decisive experimental criteria that distinguish ICT from TICT: dual fluorescence, viscosity-dependent emission, and polar-solvent quenching are the key TICT signatures. The absence of dual fluorescence and maintained emission in polar solvents in piperazine-cyanovinylene analogues argues against TICT. The piperazine ring constraint is noted as a factor that can suppress TICT relative to open-chain dialkylamino donors.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires dual fluorescence or polar-solvent quenching and viscosity-dependent emission; ICT shows single broad solvatochromic band with maintained emission. Piperazine ring constraint raises TICT state energy relative to planar ICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of DMABN and analogues; viscosity, temperature, and solvent polarity series; time-resolved emission showing dual-band kinetics for TICT systems.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong D–π–A architecture (tertiary amine donor to cyano/aza-aryl acceptors) plus a highly planar prepared conformer; S1 vertical is strongly bright (f≈1.12) consistent with an allowed conjugated excitation rather than a strongly symmetry-forbidden dark CT at the bottom of the manifold.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple torsionable junctions along the conjugated path (rotatable_bond_count=4) keep open the possibility of an excited-state twisted CT minimum that is not captured by a single-conformer vertical calculation.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Despite strong D–A features, a largely locally excited/neutral-aromatic bright state could still dominate the vertical spectrum; CT character has not yet been quantified from the orbital/charge reorganization descriptors.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Mixed LE/CT switching or conformer-dependent behavior remains plausible because conformer dependence and excited-state relaxation pathways are not yet evaluated.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No obvious intramolecular H-bonded proton donor/acceptor motif; current structure is inconsistent with ESIPT prerequisites.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the provided SMILES and shared structure prep summary, the molecule has a clear donor–acceptor architecture: a tertiary amine (N-methylpiperazine) donor attached to an extended aryl–vinyl–aryl system bearing a strong cyano acceptor substituent and a heteroaromatic pyridine-like ring (additional acceptor). The topology contains a conjugated bridge with multiple torsionable bonds (rotatable_bond_count=4; torsion_candidate_count=4), which is a structural prerequisite for potential TICT formation. However, the prepared-structure geometry proxies indicate high planarity (planarity_proxy≈0.978) and high compactness (≈0.936) for the selected conformer, which is consistent with a relatively conjugated/planar ICT-like geometry at least for one low-energy conformer. Thus, macro structural indicators support ICT feasibility (strong D–π–A pattern + near-planar conformer) while retaining TICT plausibility due to the presence of several rotatable links capable of twisting under excitation/solvation/relaxation (not evaluated here).
Capability limit note: This macro agent can only reuse the provided structure-prep summary and perform deterministic topology/geometry-proxy reasoning. It cannot compute excited-state properties, identify true minima on S1, or model solvent/temperature effects that often determine ICT vs TICT outcomes.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The Planner request maps directly to the registry action run_torsion_snapshots. Use list_rotatable_dihedrals to discover candidate dihedral(s) and then launch a bounded torsion snapshot campaign. Request a small state window including S1–S3 and a modest number of snapshots (balanced budget). Constrain selection toward conjugated donor–bridge linkages via preferred_bond_types and high relevance to pick the appropriate dihedral automatically.
Capability limit note: run_torsion_snapshots is supported by the registry and will produce snapshot vertical-state records and a torsion-sensitivity summary (energies + oscillator strengths). Per-snapshot Mulliken/dipole/population surrogates will be returned only if produced by the chosen calculation backend; absence of those surrogates will be reported explicitly. Excited-state relaxation and solvent-response/continuum effects are outside this action's validated scope and will be unsupported.
Failure policy: - If list_rotatable_dihedrals returns no high-relevance dihedral, fall back to medium relevance and/or include_peripheral=true (documenting the fallback choice).
- If one or more snapshot calculations fail, return partial results and annotate which snapshots failed and why.
- If requested charge/dipole/population surrogates are not produced by the snapshot calculations, report them as unavailable rather than inferring values.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong D–π–A scaffold; very bright lowest vertical S1 (f≈1.13); torsion scan on a key aryl–vinyl dihedral shows negligible S1 energy/oscillator variation (no bright→dark switching), which weakens a classic TICT channel along that coordinate. Direct CT metrics (e–h separation/CT number/NTOs) still missing, limiting confidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Still plausible because the molecule has multiple rotatable junctions (4 rotatable bonds) and TICT is a relaxation phenomenon; only one dihedral was scanned and state-character metrics remain proxy-only, so other twisting coordinates could still access a dark CT minimum.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual bucket remains material because (i) verifier supplementation is missing, (ii) CT descriptors are unavailable, and (iii) only one torsion coordinate has been probed; conformer/coordinate-dependent behavior could alter state ordering/brightness.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Disfavored by pronounced donor/acceptor pattern and large S0 dipole (~7.19 D) plus a strongly allowed transition (f>1), but cannot be fully excluded without fragment-localization/CT-number style metrics.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif in the structure.

working_memory.gap_trend: shrinking (we now have one negative TICT indicator on a key torsion and confirmation of a bright lowest S1; one more torsion scan can substantially reduce ambiguity).

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Down-weighted: one key conjugated torsion shows negligible sensitivity, arguing against an easily accessed dark TICT minimum along that coordinate; still viable via other rotors/relaxation.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Up slightly: state characterization remains proxy-only and only one dihedral has been probed, leaving room for alternative relaxation pathways.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Down slightly: strong D–A architecture and large dipole make a purely local neutral-aromatic excitation less likely.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Unchanged: no structural ESIPT motif.

Reasoning summary: Local reasoning summary: The requested operation maps directly to the registry action run_torsion_snapshots. The registry supports dihedral discovery (list_rotatable_dihedrals), exclusion of specific dihedral IDs, explicit angle offsets and snapshot_count, optimize_ground_state toggle, and a state_window. If calculations fail for any snapshot, report which snapshots failed and include partial results. Explicit excited-state relaxation or CT/NTO extraction failures are declared unsupported rather than retried.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong D–π–A scaffold and repeated vertical evidence that the lowest excited state is bright; two successful torsion scans on key conjugated dihedrals did not show emergence of a lower-energy dark state upon twisting. hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Still plausible because TICT is a relaxation phenomenon and multiple rotors exist; however, two torsion scans did not exhibit classic TICT bright→dark switching and the donor–aryl torsion (most diagnostic) remains untested due to a selection-policy failure.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Residual ambiguity remains because explicit CT metrics (CT number/e–h separation/Δμ/NTOs) and excited-state relaxation are unavailable; additionally, the attempted donor–aryl torsion probe failed, leaving a key discriminator unmeasured.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Disfavored by pronounced donor–acceptor architecture and strongly allowed low-lying excitation, but cannot be fully excluded without fragment-localization / CT-extent descriptors.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable proton donor/acceptor motif in the structure.

agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The requested action exactly matches the run_torsion_snapshots registry action. Use the allowed discovery action list_rotatable_dihedrals to find the donor–aryl dihedral; steer discovery toward that rotor by requesting high relevance, excluding the two specified dihedral IDs, and preferring the heteroaryl-linkage/aryl bond class. Configure snapshot_count, angle_offsets_deg, optimize_ground_state, and state_window exactly as requested. Allow bounded fallback to the next-best discovered dihedral if the explicit donor–aryl rotor cannot be found, and report which dihedral was chosen.
Capability limit note: This route supports per-snapshot vertical-state records (excitation energies and oscillator strengths) and a torsion-sensitivity summary. It does not perform excited-state relaxation or implicit solvent response; CT-number/NTO electron–hole-separation metrics are not guaranteed from this action. If the donor–aryl rotor cannot be discovered after exclusions, the tool will select the next best high-relevance rotor (or fail if none remain) and will report that selection and its justification.
Failure policy: - If list_rotatable_dihedrals finds the donor–aryl dihedral, proceed as planned.
- If the donor–aryl dihedral is not discovered but other high-relevance dihedrals remain (after exclusions), run the snapshots on the next-best candidate and report the substitution and rationale.
- If no eligible dihedral remains after applying exclusions and relevance filters, the action will not proceed and will return an explicit failure noting inability to identify a target rotor under the constraints.
- If fewer than three vertical states are available for a snapshot, report available states and flag the limitation.

last_planner_raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=ICT (0.66) vs Top2=TICT (0.18). New verifier supplementation is sufficient and high-gain: (i) no exact-identity literature found (limitation), but (ii) close-family piperazine-donor cyanovinylene and pyridine-acceptor D–π–A emitters are repeatedly assigned to planar ICT (single solvatochromic band; NTO localization donor→acceptor; moderate hole–electron separation), with explicit notes of no TICT signatures; (iii) mechanistic reviews state decisive TICT discriminators (dual fluorescence, viscosity dependence, polar-solvent quenching) and note piperazine constraints often suppress TICT. This evidence supports ICT and challenges TICT relative to prior internal ambiguity. Closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient (internal torsion scans + external close-family discriminators), even though donor–aryl torsion scan remains unexecuted.

last_planner_raw_response.contraction_reason: Finalize because verifier supplementation for ICT__vs__TICT is sufficient and provides close-family discriminators that, together with internal torsion-scan results, support ICT over TICT without needing another stalled torsion-selection attempt.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Donor (tertiary amine)–pi-conjugated acceptor motif (pyridine + nitrile) suggests an intramolecular charge-transfer excited state that can be sensitive to restriction/packing in aggregates.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple rotatable bonds (styryl-like linkage and aryl–aryl bond) can enable twisted CT minima that are quenched in solution and suppressed upon aggregation, producing AIE.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Large planar aromatic system could show aggregation-caused quenching or modest AIE via restricted intramolecular motion without dominant CT character; needs excited-state character confirmation.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Could involve mixed mechanisms (ICT+RIM, excimer/exciplex, or conformation-dependent LE/CT switching) not resolvable without basic excited-state calculations.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No obvious proton donor (e.g., phenol/amide NH) paired with an intramolecular acceptor to support ESIPT.

raw_response.diagnosis: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism (ICT vs TICT vs ESIPT vs neutral-aromatic/RIM vs unknown) for the provided molecule (C25H24N4). Working top1 is ICT and runner-up is TICT: the structure contains a strong electron-donating tertiary amine (N-methylpiperazine anilide) connected through a conjugated aryl–vinyl framework to acceptors (nitrile and a pyridine ring), which commonly yields an ICT emissive/quenched state that becomes brighter when intramolecular motions are restricted in aggregates. Top1 remains uncertain because the molecule also has several torsionable links (reported 4 rotatable bonds) that may permit a twisted CT minimum (TICT) that is dark in solution and suppressed in the solid/aggregate—classic AIE behavior. Distinguishing evidence needed: whether the lowest excited state shows strong CT character without large twisting (supports ICT) or relaxes toward a highly twisted donor–acceptor geometry with reduced oscillator strength (supports TICT). Shared prepared structure context is available (ready; 10 conformers, selected conformer id 5, SDF/XYZ paths provided). Macro can perform qualitative mechanism screening from motifs (donor/acceptor pattern, conjugation, rotors, planarity proxy) but cannot confirm excited-state ordering/oscillator strengths. Microscopic can provide bounded baseline quantum evidence (S0 optimized geometry + S1 vertical excitation and basic state character/oscillator strength) to support or weaken ICT vs TICT. First round should gather both: macro for fast chemical plausibility and microscopic for initial electronic-structure evidence while staying within low-cost constraints.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run a first-round macro qualitative mechanism screen and one bounded microscopic baseline S0/S1 calculation on the shared prepared structure to discriminate ICT vs TICT.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Using the given SMILES and shared structure summary (rotatable bonds=4, aromatic rings=3, high planarity proxy), provide a qualitative AIE-mechanism assessment focusing on ICT vs TICT vs neutral aromatic/RIM. Identify donor/acceptor units, likely CT direction, likely soft torsions enabling TICT, and any red flags for ESIPT/excimer. Do not make final decision; output key discriminative predictions (e.g., polarity dependence, solvatochromism, viscosity dependence, solid-state red/blue shift expectations).
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Perform exactly one baseline-only computation on the shared prepared structure (use prepared_structure.xyz; singlet, neutral): aTB S0 geometry optimization followed by a single-point S1 vertical excitation calculation (TD, up to 5 states). Report: S0 optimized key dihedrals across donor–pi–acceptor linkages, S1 excitation energy and oscillator strength for the brightest low state, and any available CT/LE character indicator from the tool output. Do not run conformer or torsion scans or any follow-ups.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is favored by strong donor–acceptor pattern, but TICT remains close because the conjugated bridge contains multiple torsionable bonds that can form a dark twisted CT minimum; baseline S0/S1 electronic/oscillator data and dihedral geometry will help separate them.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Macro can rapidly infer likely photophysical motifs (donor/acceptor strength, conjugation, rotor count, ESIPT prerequisites) and propose discriminative experimental signatures, but cannot confirm excited-state energetics. Microscopic (first round) can run one low-cost S0 optimization plus S1 vertical excitation to estimate excitation energy, oscillator strength, and qualitative CT character on a single conformer; it cannot yet assess conformer dependence, TICT minima, or aggregation packing/excimer formation in this round.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Rises because the molecule is a classic D–pi–A system (tertiary amine donor; nitrile/pyridyl acceptors) likely supporting strong intramolecular charge transfer.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Stays competitive because several rotatable bonds can enable a twisted CT geometry that is quenched in solution and restricted in aggregates.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Falls strongly due to absence of an obvious intramolecular H-bonded proton donor required for ESIPT.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Limited because donor/acceptor substitution suggests CT physics beyond a purely neutral aromatic LE/RIM picture, though RIM may still contribute.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Remains nonzero to cover mixed or non-classical behaviors (LE/CT switching, excimer/exciplex) not determinable without more computations/experiments.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Rises because the molecule is a classic D–pi–A system (tertiary amine donor; nitrile/pyridyl acceptors) likely supporting strong intramolecular charge transfer.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Stays competitive because several rotatable bonds can enable a twisted CT geometry that is quenched in solution and restricted in aggregates.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Falls strongly due to absence of an obvious intramolecular H-bonded proton donor required for ESIPT.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Limited because donor/acceptor substitution suggests CT physics beyond a purely neutral aromatic LE/RIM picture, though RIM may still contribute.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Remains nonzero to cover mixed or non-classical behaviors (LE/CT switching, excimer/exciplex) not determinable without more computations/experiments.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=ICT (0.46) vs Top2=TICT (0.33). Latest evidence modestly strengthens ICT: macro shows a strong donor–acceptor layout and a highly planar conformer (planarity_proxy≈0.978), and microscopic baseline shows the lowest excited state is already strongly bright (E≈3.42 eV, f≈1.12), consistent with an allowed conjugated excitation/ICT-like transition rather than a primarily dark CT manifold at the vertical point. TICT remains a close challenger because the structure still has several torsionable bonds (4) that could enable twisting after excitation/relaxation, which this baseline vertical calculation cannot test. An additional candidate affected: neutral aromatic is slightly weakened because the D–π–A motif and large dipole (≈7.19 D) argue against purely neutral behavior, but it cannot be ruled out without explicit CT descriptors. Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether S1 relaxes to/near a twisted CT minimum (large donor–acceptor decoupling, reduced oscillator strength/emissive rate) versus staying relatively planar with emissive ICT. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still missing. Best next action is a single bounded microscopic task that directly measures CT character on S1 and provides a sharper ICT vs TICT discriminator before spending budget on external verification.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run ONE bounded microscopic action: extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle on artifact_bundle_id='round_01_baseline_bundle' (from round_01). Deliver: S1 charge-transfer descriptors (e.g., electron–hole separation / centroid distance if available, Δq donor/acceptor partition metrics, transition density localization proxies) for state_index=1 (and optionally compare to state_index=2 if included by the extractor). Goal: determine whether the bright S1 is predominantly CT (supporting ICT) or more locally excited and whether there is evidence of strong donor/acceptor separation that would make a TICT relaxation plausible.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute registry action extract_ct_descriptors_from_bundle using existing artifacts only (no new quantum calculations): bundle_id=round_01_baseline_bundle; focus on state_index=1 (S1) primarily; return CT metrics that can distinguish an ICT-like bright CT/partial-CT state from a more LE state that could precede TICT relaxation.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is favored structurally and by a strongly bright lowest vertical excitation, but TICT cannot be excluded because twisting is an excited-state relaxation phenomenon not probed by single-geometry vertical data; CT character metrics from the computed states are the nearest-term discriminator.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Macro evidence is strong for donor–acceptor topology and torsionability but cannot decide excited-state relaxation. Microscopic baseline provided vertical energies/oscillator strengths but not explicit CT descriptors; the next microscopic parsing/extraction action can add high-value CT character evidence at low cost.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: this is round 1 with new macro + microscopic baseline evidence gathered.
raw_response.contraction_reason: Prefer a low-cost discriminator (CT descriptor extraction) before external verifier or heavier conformer/torsion scans.
raw_response.evidence_summary: Macro: strong D–π–A motif; rotatable_bond_count=4; planarity_proxy≈0.978 suggests a planar low-energy conformer consistent with ICT feasibility. raw_response.main_gap: Lack of explicit excited-state charge-transfer character metrics and lack of any evidence about post-excitation twisting/relaxation toward a TICT minimum.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 2 status (budget: 2/20 used; 19 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.44) vs Top2=TICT (0.34). Latest evidence leaves the ICT lead largely unresolved rather than strengthened: the attempted CT-descriptor extraction could not recover key CT discriminators (electron–hole separation, CT number, density overlap, Δdipole, NTO occupations), so we gained little mechanistic separation beyond prior macro+baseline vertical data. This also leaves the TICT challenger effectively unchanged because the main missing discriminator (does S1 have strong CT character and/or does twisting strongly alter state ordering/brightness?) is still missing. A third candidate affected: neutral aromatic remains low but not further reduced, because without CT descriptors we cannot conclusively exclude an LE-dominated bright S1. Verifier supplementation is still missing; closure justification is still collecting (internal) but currently blocked from progressing via parse-only CT extraction. Best next action is a Verifier query targeted to ICT vs TICT discriminators for D–π–A styryl-biaryl/pyridyl cyano systems with tertiary amine donors, including expected experimental signatures (solvent polarity dependence, viscosity dependence, dual emission, TICT quenching) and structural red flags (rotor placement) that would challenge the current ICT-leaning picture.
raw_response.task_instruction: Verifier task (external evidence) for target pair ICT__vs__TICT: For a D–π–A molecule matching SMILES CN1CCN(c2ccc(C=C(C#N)c3ccc(-c4ccncc4)cc3)cc2)CC1 (tertiary amine donor; styryl-aryl bridge; cyano + pyridyl acceptors; multiple rotors), find literature precedents and discriminator criteria separating ICT vs TICT behavior. Report: (1) common experimental signatures of TICT formation (polarity/viscosity dependence, dual emission, large Stokes shift with low quantum yield, temperature dependence) vs planar ICT (single band, strong solvatochromism but less viscosity dependence); (2) whether closely related piperazine/amine–styryl–aryl–CN/pyridyl dyes are known to form TICT; (3) any structural features here (rotor positions, conjugation continuity, donor strength) that would strongly favor or disfavor TICT. Provide challenge signals that would overturn an ICT assumption.

decision.diagnosis: Round 4/20 (17 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.44) vs Top2=TICT (0.32). Latest evidence modestly strengthens ICT relative to TICT: the new torsion-snapshot scan over a high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral (dih_11_12_13_14; 0/30/60/90°) shows essentially no torsion sensitivity (excitation spread ~0.0059 eV; oscillator spread ~0.0004) and no emergence of a low-energy dark state upon twisting in that coordinate, which weakens a classic TICT bright→dark switching narrative. However, closure remains unresolved because (i) only one dihedral was probed, (ii) snapshots are vertical (no excited-state relaxation), and (iii) explicit CT character metrics (e.g., Δμ, NTOs, CT numbers) are still unavailable. Verifier supplementation remains missing (prior connection error). Closure justification is still collecting but no longer fully blocked: we now have one torsion-dependent discriminator, yet it is incomplete. Best next action is another bounded microscopic discriminator targeting state character directly (localized orbitals/NTO-like surrogates) on the baseline and/or torsion bundles to assess donor→acceptor charge separation versus locally excited character.
decision.task_instruction: Execute ONE bounded microscopic registry action: run_targeted_state_characterization on artifact_bundle_id='round_01_baseline_bundle' (preferred) to extract any available state-character descriptors for S1 (and S2 if available), including at minimum: dominant transition localization/assignment (donor vs acceptor fragments), change in charge distribution proxies if available, and any available excited-state dipole/CT surrogates. Goal: decide whether the bright S1 is predominantly ICT-like (donor→acceptor CT/mixed CT) or largely locally excited (which would leave room for subsequent TICT upon relaxation).

decision.capability_assessment: Microscopic capability successfully produced torsion-dependent vertical-state data (energies, oscillator strengths) and a torsion-sensitivity summary; however, it still cannot perform excited-state relaxation/solvent response, and prior parse-only CT extraction could not retrieve electron–hole/NTO descriptors. decision.stagnation_assessment: Stagnation is reduced this round: unlike rounds 2–3, we obtained new discriminative internal evidence (torsion insensitivity for a key dihedral). raw_response.diagnosis: Round budget: 5/20 used; 16 rounds remaining. Top1=ICT (0.47) vs Top2=TICT (0.28). Latest evidence mildly strengthens ICT relative to TICT: targeted state characterization confirms an intense lowest S1 (3.415 eV, f=1.1266) dominated by a single HOMO→LUMO-like excitation (101→102), consistent with a robust allowed excitation rather than a torsion-activated dark-state signature. However, the state-characterization remains proxy-only (no transition localization, no CT-number/e–h separation/Δμ/NTOs), so it does not decisively quantify CT character. TICT remains the main challenger because multiple rotors exist and only one aryl–vinyl dihedral was scanned previously; the key remaining discriminator is whether twisting along other junctions (notably donor–aryl and/or aryl–heteroaryl) induces a low-energy dark CT state (bright→dark switching) consistent with a TICT relaxation pathway. Verifier supplementation remains missing (connection error) and closure justification is still collecting (internal evidence improved but not sufficient). Best next action is an additional internal pairwise-discriminative torsion snapshot scan targeting a different high-relevance dihedral to directly test for a TICT-accessible dark state on an alternative twisting coordinate.
raw_response.task_instruction: Execute ONE bounded microscopic registry action: run_torsion_snapshots, but target a DIFFERENT high-relevance dihedral than the already-scanned aryl–vinyl dihedral (exclude dihedral_id='dih_11_12_13_14' if the tool allows exclusion). Prefer the donor–aryl dihedral (amine/piperazine-attached aryl bond) or the aryl–heteroaryl (phenyl–pyridyl) linkage. Use optimize_ground_state=false. Use ~4 snapshots spanning 0/30/60/90° and a small vertical state window (S1–S3). Deliver per-snapshot excitation energies and oscillator strengths, and report whether a low-energy dark (very low-f) state emerges upon twisting (bright→dark switching) and any available charge/dipole/population surrogates.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Run exactly one Amesp registry action: run_torsion_snapshots. Select a high-relevance conjugated dihedral different from dih_11_12_13_14 (prefer donor–aryl or aryl–heteroaryl). If explicit exclusion is supported, exclude dih_11_12_13_14. Set optimize_ground_state=false. Use 4 snapshots across 0/30/60/90 degrees (or closest default). Compute vertical states S1–S3 and return per-snapshot energies + oscillator strengths; flag any emergence of low-energy dark state upon twisting.

Transition localization and explicit CT metrics (CT-number/e–h separation/NTOs/Δμ) were not produced (proxy-only characterization). Prior torsion snapshot evidence: for aryl–vinyl dihedral dih_11_12_13_14 (0/30/60/90°), torsion_sensitivity=False with excitation_spread ~0.0059 eV and oscillator_spread ~0.0004 (no bright→dark switching). raw_response.main_gap: ICT vs TICT closure still lacks (1) explicit excited-state CT character metrics (CT number, electron–hole separation, Δμ, NTOs) and (2) torsion-dependent state-ordering/brightness evidence on OTHER key rotors (donor–aryl, aryl–heteroaryl) that could enable a TICT dark minimum upon relaxation.

raw_response.gap_trend: shrinking (we now have one negative TICT indicator on a key torsion and confirmation of a bright lowest S1; one more torsion scan can substantially reduce ambiguity).

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 6/20 (15 rounds remaining): Top1=ICT (0.49) vs Top2=TICT (0.24). Latest evidence (new torsion snapshot scan on a different high-relevance aryl–aryl dihedral, dih_17_18_21_22) modestly strengthens ICT and weakens TICT: across 0/30/60/90° the lowest state remains the bright state (lowest_state_is_bright) with no emergence of a distinctly low-energy dark state that would signal a classic TICT channel along this coordinate. While this dihedral is torsion-sensitive in energy (excitation spread ~0.23 eV), brightness does not collapse, which is less consistent with a TICT sink becoming accessible upon twisting. This pushes probability mass away from TICT and toward ICT (and some residual to unknown because CT/NTO descriptors and relaxation are still missing). Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs TICT: whether excited-state relaxation (not probed) can access a twisted CT minimum on other dihedrals (e.g., donor–aryl) that becomes dark/low-energy, plus direct CT extent metrics (e–h separation/CT number/Δμ/NTOs). Verifier supplementation remains missing (connection errors; no external precedent), and closure justification is still collecting but not sufficient. Best next action is another bounded internal discriminator: a torsion snapshot scan targeting the donor–aryl dihedral (most directly connected to donor–acceptor decoupling) to test for bright→dark switching that would more strongly support TICT if present.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=ICT (0.66) vs Top2=TICT (0.18).
